question: What is the in-addr.arpa name used to look up PTR records for the IPv4 address 192.0.2.10?
explanation: IPv4 PTR lookups byte-reverse the address before appending in-addr.arpa. So 192.0.2.10 becomes 10.2.0.192.in-addr.arpa.

question: An SMTP receiver performs an FCrDNS check on a connection from IP 203.0.113.5. Which sequence of lookups must close the loop?
options: A. A record first, then PTR record, then TXT record
B. PTR (5.113.0.203.in-addr.arpa.) -> name, then A (name) -> same IP 203.0.113.5
C. MX record, then A record, then PTR record
D. Only the PTR record needs to exist; the A lookup is optional
explanation: Forward-confirmed reverse DNS closes the loop: PTR(name) -> name, then A(name) -> same IP. RFC 5321 §5.1 makes this a SHOULD-level check used by major mail providers.

question: What is the ip6.arpa name used to look up PTR records for the IPv6 address 2001:db8::1?
explanation: IPv6 reverses by nibble (4-bit half-byte), not by byte. Expanding 2001:db8::1 to its full 32-nibble form and reversing gives the long dotted name shown in option B (RFC 3596).
